name: FB Live Comments
overview:
Facebook Live Comments is a real-time commenting system for live video streams. Viewers can post
comments that are immediately visible to all other viewers of the same stream. The system must handle
massive fan-out (a popular stream may have millions of concurrent viewers), deliver comments with <500ms
latency, handle comment moderation, and gracefully degrade when a stream goes viral. The write rate can
spike to 100k comments/second for major events.

inScope: Post a comment on a live stream
Receive new comments in real-time (<500ms latency)

items: Support 10M concurrent viewers across all streams
Comment delivery latency <500ms for online viewers
Handle 100k comments/sec during peak events (major sports, concerts)
99.9% availability; brief comment delays are tolerable over full outages
Eventual consistency for comment delivery — slight reordering acceptable; strong consistency not required
Fan-out must scale linearly: viral streams with 1M+ viewers must not degrade other streams

entities.name: Comment
entities.schema:
{
  comment_id: UUID,
  stream_id: string,
  user_id: string,

  username: string,
  text: string (max 500 chars),
  timestamp: ISO8601,
  status: enum(VISIBLE|HIDDEN|DELETED)
}

entities.name: Stream
entities.schema:
{
  stream_id: string,
  broadcaster_id: string,
  title: string,
  status: enum(LIVE|ENDED),
  viewer_count: int,
  started_at: timestamp
}

approaches.title: Polling
approaches.description: Each viewer polls GET /comments every second. 10M viewers × 1 req/sec = 10M req/sec. Even with caching, this is unsustainable. 1-second polling also means up to 1-second delay.

approaches.title: WebSocket per Viewer
approaches.description: Each viewer maintains a WebSocket connection. Comments are pushed directly. 10M concurrent WebSocket connections require ~10k connection servers (1k connections/server). Fan-out: each comment must be sent to all viewers of that stream.
approaches.tradeoff: Fan-out for viral streams (1M viewers) is expensive; connection servers are stateful.

approaches.title: WebSocket + Pub/Sub Fan-out
approaches.description: Viewers connect to WebSocket servers. Each WebSocket server subscribes to a Redis pub/sub channel per stream_id. When a comment is posted, it's published to the stream's Redis channel. All WebSocket servers subscribed to that channel receive it and push to their connected viewers. For viral streams (>100k viewers), use a tiered fan-out: Kafka → regional fan-out services → Redis pub/sub → WebSocket servers.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis pub/sub doesn't persist messages; late joiners miss comments (handled by separate

approaches.title: recent-comments API).
approaches.description: Comment Storage MySQL with stream_id Index Store all comments in MySQL, indexed by stream_id + timestamp. At 100k comments/sec, MySQL write throughput is exceeded. Queries for recent comments require full index scans.
approaches.tradeoff: Write throughput too low; query performance degrades with table size.

approaches.title: Redis Sorted Set per Stream
approaches.description: Store comments in a Redis sorted set (ZADD) with timestamp as score. ZREVRANGE returns the last N comments. Sub-millisecond reads and writes. But Redis is in-memory; storing all comments for all streams is expensive. No persistence for post-stream replay.
approaches.tradeoff: Memory cost for long streams; no durable storage.

approaches.title: Redis Sorted Set (hot) + Cassandra (cold)
approaches.description: Redis sorted set stores the last 200 comments per stream (sliding window). Cassandra stores all comments durably, partitioned by stream_id with clustering key = timestamp. On join, the client fetches the last 50 comments from Redis (fast). Historical queries go to Cassandra. Comments are written to both Redis and Cassandra asynchronously via Kafka.
approaches.tradeoff: Dual-write complexity; slight inconsistency window between Redis and Cassandra.

components.name: Comment API
components.responsibility: Validates and accepts comment posts; publishes to Kafka; returns 201

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Durable comment event stream; partitioned by stream_id

components.name: Fan-out Service
components.responsibility: Consumes Kafka; publishes to Redis pub/sub channels; handles tiered fan-out for viral streams

components.name: WebSocket Servers
components.responsibility: Maintain viewer connections; subscribe to Redis pub/sub; push comments to viewers

components.name: Redis Pub/Sub
components.responsibility: Per-stream channels for real-time comment distribution

components.name: Redis Sorted Set
components.responsibility: Stores last 200 comments per stream for fast recent-comment queries

components.name: Cassandra
components.responsibility: Durable comment store; partition key = stream_id; clustering key = timestamp DESC

components.name: Moderation Service
components.responsibility: Scans comments for policy violations; updates comment status in

questions.title: 2. Comment Ordering
questions.content:
Comments must appear in chronological order. Each comment is assigned a timestamp by the Comment API
server. However, clock skew between servers can cause out-of-order delivery. Solution: use a hybrid logical
clock (HLC) that combines physical time with a logical counter. HLC timestamps are monotonically increasing
even across servers with clock skew, ensuring consistent ordering.

questions.title: 3. Moderation at Scale
questions.content:
The Moderation Service uses a combination of: (1) keyword blocklist (synchronous, <1ms), (2) ML classifier for
hate speech/spam (async, <100ms), and (3) human review queue for borderline cases. Comments are shown
immediately (optimistic display) and hidden retroactively if the ML classifier flags them. This gives the
appearance of real-time posting while maintaining safety.

questions.title: 4. Connection Management
questions.content:
WebSocket servers are stateless with respect to comment content — they only maintain connection state
(user_id → socket). When a viewer connects, the WebSocket server registers the connection in Redis (user_id
→ server_id mapping). When a viewer disconnects, the registration is removed. This allows the fan-out service
to route targeted messages (e.g., moderation actions) to the correct WebSocket server.

questions.title: 5. Backpressure and Rate Limiting
questions.content:
Each user is rate-limited to 1 comment per 2 seconds (Redis token bucket per user_id). The Comment API
rejects excess comments with 429 Too Many Requests. For the fan-out pipeline, Kafka provides natural
backpressure: if the fan-out service falls behind, Kafka buffers the comments. The WebSocket servers
implement client-side flow control: if a viewer's connection is slow, comments are batched and sent in bursts.

keyFlows: ① Viewer posts comment → Comment API → Kafka
② Fan-out Svc consumes Kafka → publishes to Redis Pub/Sub channel
③ WebSocket Svc subscribes to channel → pushes to all viewers
⑤ Moderation Svc scans Kafka → hides/deletes in Cassandra + Redis

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Redis pub/sub vs. Redis Streams: pub/sub is fire-and-forget (no persistence, no
consumer groups), while Streams provide durable, replayable message queues — understand when to use
each.
Expert Note: Learn about WebSocket connection scaling: how to use consistent hashing to route viewers
to the same WebSocket server, and how to handle server restarts without dropping connections.
Expert Note: Understand hybrid logical clocks (HLC): how they combine physical timestamps with logical
counters to provide causally consistent ordering across distributed nodes.
Expert Note: Study comment sampling algorithms for viral streams: how to select a representative sample
of comments while ensuring the broadcaster sees all comments.
Expert Note: Learn about the fan-out-on-write vs. fan-out-on-read trade-off: why live comments use
fan-out-on-write (push to all viewers immediately) while news feeds use fan-out-on-read for celebrity
accounts.

Facebook Live Comments
